name: Bes
description: Bes was an ancient Egyptian dwarf god, a protector of households, mothers, children, and childbirth. Unlike most Egyptian gods who were typically depicted in profile, Bes was almost always shown frontally, often with a lion's mane and tail. He was a complex figure, both fearsome and benevolent. His grotesque appearance, including his large head, protruding tongue, and bandy legs, was intended to frighten away evil spirits and protect those under his care. Bes was not associated with a specific temple or cult center, but rather was a popular deity whose image was found in homes throughout Egypt, often adorning furniture, mirrors, and cosmetic containers. He was believed to ward off bad luck, bring good fortune, and ensure a safe and happy life for families. Bes's role extended beyond the domestic sphere; he was also associated with music, dance, and revelry, and was sometimes depicted playing musical instruments. He was a guardian of sleep, preventing nightmares and ensuring peaceful rest. His popularity endured for centuries, reflecting the Egyptian people's deep concern for the well-being of their families and the importance of protection from unseen forces.
subtitle: Protector of Home and Family

keyMyths.title: Bes Protects Childbirth
keyMyths.description: Bes is believed to ward off evil spirits during childbirth, ensuring a safe delivery for the mother and child. His image was often depicted in birthing rooms. This protection helps with a healthy start.

keyMyths.title: Bes and Music
keyMyths.description: Bes is often shown playing musical instruments, such as the tambourine or harp, bringing joy and celebration. He's a guardian of good times. This joyful aspect of Bes is very important.

keyMyths.title: Bes Wards Off Evil
keyMyths.description: Bes's grotesque appearance is intended to frighten away evil spirits and protect the home from harm. He is a powerful protector. This role of protector is his main function.

sources.author: Geraldine Pinch
sources.work: Egyptian Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Goddesses, and Traditions

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt
